parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: It started a few days ago when I was just sitting in my car in the driveway, completely fine one second and then suddenly my chest felt tight. I tried to shake it off and just walk inside, but my hands wouldn't stop trembling. I kept telling myself it was nothing, just a weird glitch in my system. Now I find myself checking my pulse every time I sit down, waiting for that weird heaviness to come back. It’s honestly just exhausting to feel so on edge over nothing at all.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you’re going through something really unsettling, and I want you to know that what you’re feeling is valid. That sudden tightness in your chest and the trembling can be incredibly frightening, especially when it comes out of nowhere. It’s completely understandable to feel on edge afterward, almost like you’re waiting for it to happen again.

Many people experience moments like this, and it can be a sign of anxiety or a panic response, even if there’s no obvious trigger. The exhaustion you’re feeling is real—being in a state of heightened alertness takes a lot out of you.

Have you considered talking to a healthcare professional about what happened? Sometimes just having a doctor check things out can bring peace of mind, and they can help you understand what might be going on. In the meantime, gentle breathing exercises or grounding techniques might help when you feel that heaviness returning. You’re not alone in this, and it’s okay to reach out for support.

turns.user_message: That same heavy feeling from the driveway keeps creeping back whenever I try to settle into my usual routine. I catch myself holding my breath without realizing it, like I’m bracing for a wave that never actually crashes. My thoughts start moving a mile a minute, jumping from one small worry to the next until everything feels completely out of control. I’ve started pacing around the room just to burn off this restless energy that won’t quiet down. It’s frustrating because I know logically there’s no real danger, but my body just refuses to listen.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly overwhelming, and it takes a lot of self-awareness to notice those patterns in your body and mind. It’s like your nervous system is stuck in a loop, preparing for something that isn’t there—and that disconnect between logic and sensation can be so frustrating.

Sometimes when the body holds onto tension like that, it’s because it hasn’t had a chance to complete the stress cycle. Pacing is actually a natural response—it’s your body trying to move through that trapped energy. Have you ever tried pairing movement with intentional breathing? Something as simple as shaking out your hands, stretching, or even a few minutes of walking outside while focusing on exhaling fully can sometimes help signal to your nervous system that it’s okay to ease up.

Would you feel comfortable sharing what usually helps you feel grounded, even a little? Or is this feeling new in how persistent it is?

turns.user_message: The driveway moment feels like it’s happening on repeat now, except the waves are hitting harder and leaving me completely drained. I can barely sit still for ten minutes before my heart starts pounding and my vision gets all fuzzy around the edges. Every tiny sound makes me jump, and I feel like I’m constantly waiting for the floor to drop out from under me. My whole body is just stuck in overdrive, turning ordinary quiet moments into this intense, suffocating pressure. I just want to feel steady again, but everything inside me is vibrating with this relentless, unshakable tension.
turns.model_response:
That sounds incredibly overwhelming and exhausting. What you're describing—the constant state of high alert, the physical symptoms, the feeling of being unable to settle—sounds like your nervous system is stuck in a prolonged state of fight-or-flight. It’s not just in your head; your body is reacting as if it’s under constant threat, even when things seem quiet on the outside.

It’s okay to feel this way, and it’s also okay to acknowledge how draining it is. You deserve relief, and you don’t have to carry this alone. Have you been able to talk to anyone about what you’re experiencing—a trusted friend, family member, or a professional who can help you navigate this? Sometimes, having support can make the weight feel a little lighter.

In the meantime, if you’re open to it, small grounding practices might help bring a tiny bit of steadiness back—things like focusing on your breath, feeling your feet on the floor, or naming things you can see, hear, and touch. But please know that it’s okay if that feels too hard right now. You’re doing the best you can in a really tough moment.

I’m here to listen, whenever you need. You’re not alone in this.
